figures.caption: Fig. 4: Functional patterns and failure modes detected by SceneTeract. (a) navigation maps highlighting restricted areas (yellow) disconnected from the main area (green) for agents with larger occupancy radii (impacting the red border); (b) a blocked hallway prevents access to the target cabinet, even though its local interaction clearance (green box) is valid; (c) insufficient space (red box) to articulate the object; (d) although the object is partially reachable, the identified interaction area (orange) to sit on the couch is obstructed; (e) the identified interactive space (pink) to open the cabinet is within the agent’s reach from a valid interaction zone (blue).
Table 6: Hyperparameters used for GRPO post-training.

Fig. 5: Main components of the SceneTeract framework implementation. The system is organized around four interacting modules: a Scene Manager (which maintains structured scene state and object properties), a Scene Renderer (which produces visual observations for prompting), a VLM Client (which instantiates the Semantic Planner), and a Geometric Verifier (which executes grounded feasibility checks). The modules communicate via the shared input triplet I = ⟨S, T ,A⟩ and the action-to-property mapping Req(a) that links each atomic action to the relevant geometric checks.

Fig. 1: We introduce SceneTeract, a verification engine that, given a 3D scene, an embodied agent profile, and a target activity, decomposes the task into atomic actions and validates each step with explicit geometric and physical checks, producing finegrained feasibility diagnostics. We deploy this pipeline for three applications: auditing synthetic 3D scenes, benchmarking frontier VLMs on embodiment-aware functional reasoning, and providing reward signals for post-training a VLM in order to improve its reasoning capabilities.
Table 2: Atomic actions to boolean properties mapping. The left panel defines the boolean properties verified by the grounding engine. The right panel maps them to

Fig. 10: Verification results for the has_Clearance property. To simulate articulation and manipulation, objects coupled with an atomic action from the Handling family Ah require a collision-free clearance volume facing their interactive side.

Table 7: Keyword mapping for the Spatial Reasoning Reward (rspatial). A reward is triggered if the model’s generated <think> trace contains any of the target keywords corresponding to the specific physical failure identified by the verification engine.

Fig. 12: Perceptual study interface. The interface provides comprehensive context for each execution trace, including the target activity, the agent profile, a top-down visual rendering of the scene, and various debug images generated by the grounding engine. Evaluation is conducted by reviewing the logical soundness of the VLM-generated plan, verifying agreement with the system’s geometric pass/fail verdicts at both the atomic action step level and the overall task level, and finally rating the utility of the generated actionable insight.

Fig. 9: Object functional parts identification and 3D deprojection. On the top row, from left to right: object view used to prompt Molmo [11], predicted interaction point(s) for the intended action, segmentation mask derived by SAM [30]. On the bottom row: in-context image of the object featuring the deprojected interactable volume (pink).

Fig. 2: Overview of the SceneTeract verification framework. Given an input 3D scene (S), embodied agent profile (A), and target activity (T ), a VLM planner (Φ) decomposes the activity into atomic actions, which are then checked by a 3D verifier (Ψ) for geometric feasibility assessment. The pipeline returns a fine-grained affordance report (R) with step-level pass/fail diagnostics. This enables multiple downstream applications (Section 3.4), including scene auditing, VLM benchmarking, and training-time reward supervision.
